title: 1 Chronicles 15
subtitle: The Ark Brought to Jerusalem — Properly

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Ark Brought to Jerusalem

sections.header: Verses 1–15 — David Prepares the Levites to Carry the Ark Correctly

refs.note: The Kohathites are designated to carry holy objects — the law David now follows.
“Insert the poles into the rings on the sides of the ark to carry it.” No carts. Poles and Levitical shoulders.

notes.note: David’s admission of error teaches that even good leaders make mistakes, and the mark of godliness is not perfection but willingness to learn. The king who failed in chapter 13 succeeds in chapter 15 because he submits to God’s word. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: bĕmottōt (“with poles”) on their shoulders (biktēpām) — exactly as prescribed in Num 7:9 and Exod 25:14. The Chronicler uses the technical vocabulary of the Priestly legislation. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: David learns from the Uzzah disaster. He prepares a tent for the ark, then declares: “No one but the Levites may carry the ark of God, because the LORD chose them.” He assembles 862 Levites from the three main families (Kohath, Merari, Gershon) and three additional Levitical clans. David explains: “The LORD our God broke out against us because we did not inquire of him about how to do it in the prescribed way.” The lesson is explicit: zeal without knowledge is dangerous; the method matters.

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew tôlĕḏôt (generations) is a structuring device that traces the thread of God's purpose through family lines. From Genesis to Chronicles, genealogies are not mere lists but theological arguments: they demonstrate continuity, establish legitimacy, and show that God's covenant promises pass from generation to generation.

sections.header: Verses 16–29 — Music and Celebration; the Procession Succeeds

refs.note: The Samuel parallel includes the quarrel with Michal; the Chronicler stops at the despising.
“Praise the LORD with the sounding of the trumpet, with harp and lyre... Let everything that has breath praise the LORD.” The full-spectrum worship described here.

notes.note: Michal’s contempt for David’s dancing warns against the spirit of religious sophistication that despises genuine worship. Those who are too dignified to worship wholeheartedly are spiritually poorer than those who dance. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The “linen ephod” (ʾēpōd bad) is priestly clothing (cf. 1 Sam 2:18; 22:18). David’s wearing it blurs the line between king and priest — a deliberate theological statement about David’s liturgical role. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: David appoints Levitical musicians for the procession: singers, instrumentalists, and gatekeepers. Heman, Asaph, and Ethan lead with bronze cymbals; others play harps and lyres. Kenaniah, the head Levite, directs the music because “he was skillful at it.” The ark enters Jerusalem with shouting, trumpets, cymbals, harps, and lyres. David dances, wearing a linen ephod. Michal (Saul’s daughter) watches from a window and “despises him in her heart.” The Chronicler preserves this detail from Samuel but omits the subsequent quarrel — the positive event takes priority.

heb.paragraph: The Hebrew qōḏeš (holiness) denotes separation for divine purpose. In the context of temple worship, it marks the distinction between common and sacred — a boundary that defines Israel's entire liturgical life. The Chronicler's emphasis on holiness reflects his concern for proper worship as the foundation of national life.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVAfter David had constructed buildings for himself in the City of David, he prepared a place for the ark of God and pitched a tent for it.ESVDavid built houses for himself in the city of David. And he prepared a place for the ark of God and pitched a tent for it.NIVDavid assembled all Israel in Jerusalem to bring up the ark of the Lord to the place he had prepared for it.ESVAnd David assembled all Israel at Jerusalem to bring up the ark of the LORD to its place, which he had prepared for it.
